coredata.dc:title: 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(p-tolyl)porphyrin derived carbon dots as colorant in flexo and screen inks with multi-level covert features for security printing

coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent inks with covert security features, which make it difficult for forgers to replicate, are highly sought after among the array of anti-counterfeit technologies. The present study focuses on the synthesis of Carbon dots (CDs) from 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(p-tolyl)porphyrin (TTP) via a solvothermal approach. The prepared CDs exhibit attractive emissive characteristics with an absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 38.85 %. Time-resolved fluorescence studies reveal the multiple exponential decay nature of the emitters, which could be due to the diverse chemical environment on the surface of the CDs. XPS analysis proves oxygen as the major element, along with carbon, sulfur, and nitrogen as minor elements. DFT studies on four optimized structures of CDs along with their respective UV and FT-IR plots are conducted. The theoretically derived photoluminescence plots corresponding to the 25-30th excited states coincide with the experimental emission peak. CDs demonstrate characteristic variations in fluorescence in the presence of water, attributed to π-π stacking induced aggregation. Aggregate formation is also influenced by pH changes, affecting the surface functional groups in proximity to the solvent medium. CDs are used as colorants in the fabrication of both flexo and screen inks. Flexo ink retains the fluorescence features of the CDs, while screen ink offers multilevel security features. Exposing UV-dull paper to 365 nm UV light allows the ink inscribed letters to appear fluorescent blue, whereas the use of UV/green optical filters facilitates the visualization of green/yellow emissions. The introduction of multilevel features makes the duplication of the print difficult, protecting the integrity of the product and could be used for real-life applications.

head.ce:title: 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(p-tolyl)porphyrin derived carbon dots as colorant in flexo and screen inks with multi-level covert features for security printing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent inks with covert security features, which make it difficult for forgers to replicate, are highly sought after among the array of anti-counterfeit technologies. The present study focuses on the synthesis of Carbon dots (CDs) from 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(p-tolyl)porphyrin (TTP) via a solvothermal approach. The prepared CDs exhibit attractive emissive characteristics with an absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 38.85 %. Time-resolved fluorescence studies reveal the multiple exponential decay nature of the emitters, which could be due to the diverse chemical environment on the surface of the CDs. XPS analysis proves oxygen as the major element, along with carbon, sulfur, and nitrogen as minor elements. DFT studies on four optimized structures of CDs along with their respective UV and FT-IR plots are conducted. The theoretically derived photoluminescence plots corresponding to the 25-30th excited states coincide with the experimental emission peak. CDs demonstrate characteristic variations in fluorescence in the presence of water, attributed to π-π stacking induced aggregation. Aggregate formation is also influenced by pH changes, affecting the surface functional groups in proximity to the solvent medium. CDs are used as colorants in the fabrication of both flexo and screen inks. Flexo ink retains the fluorescence features of the CDs, while screen ink offers multilevel security features. Exposing UV-dull paper to 365 nm UV light allows the ink inscribed letters to appear fluorescent blue, whereas the use of UV/green optical filters facilitates the visualization of green/yellow emissions. The introduction of multilevel features makes the duplication of the print difficult, protecting the integrity of the product and could be used for real-life applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the realm of security and authenticity, the ever-evolving techniques of counterfeiting present a significant challenge for governments, businesses, and consumers [1–8]. However, advancements in material science and printing technologies offer a promising path for tackling this persistent threat, paving the way for more efficient and cost-effective solutions [9–13]. The use of fluorescent inks, which combine effectiveness with affordability presents a compelling practical alternative for safeguarding valuable documents and products, thereby ensuring trust and integrity across various sectors. The prints obtained using specially formulated inks remain invisible under normal lighting conditions but fluoresce brightly when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) or specific visible light wavelengths. This unique property enables the creation of covert markings, codes, or logos that are invisible to the naked eye but become readily visible under UV/visible light illumination. Researchers have explored the potential of various dyes and pigments as fluorescent colorants in these prints, offering a versatile and cost-effective approach to enhance security features in diverse documents and products. By harnessing the inherent smart features of intelligent colorant materials, these prints can be customized to specific requirements while retaining their covert nature [14–26]. A variety of fluorescent materials including metal quantum dots [9], lanthanide doped phosphors [27,28], perovskite quantum dots [29,30], and organic luminophores [31] are widely investigated as colorants in fluorescent inks. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbon nanomaterials exhibiting zero-dimension with tunable photoluminescent property. They are facile to synthesize due to the availability of cheap precursors [32]. They are extensively used in various applications such as bioimaging [33], targeted drug delivery [34], metal-ion sensing [35] etc.

5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(p-tolyl)porphyrin (TTP) that belong to the macrocyclic porphyrin family consists of four pyrrole rings linked by methine bridges, each adorned with four p-tolyl (4-methylphenyl) groups. Previous reports state synthesis of dimeric salophen platinum (II) complexes capable of displaying deep red emission in solid state [36]. Moreover, researchers utilized derivatives of Porphyrin for the synthesis of red-emissive CDs via hydrothermal approach [37,38]. As CDs possess tunable fluorescence properties for anti-counterfeit applications [39], TTP is investigated as a precursor material for the synthesis of these carbonaceous nanoparticles with the aim of offering a facile and intelligent solution as a functional colorant in fluorescent security ink formulations. TTP-derived CDs are prepared through a solvothermal method and subsequently purified through column chromatography. The CDs of interest that exhibit good fluorescence quantum yield (QY) are harnessed as a fluorescent functional colorant for the formulation of water-based flexo and solvent-based screen inks. The incorporation of CDs in flexo ink conferred print proofs with better photostability and wear resistance. Since counterfeit products are rampant and easy to duplicate due to advanced technology, multi-level security inks have an edge to counter such menace. Interestingly, the CDs in screen ink formulation printed on UV dull paper could display unique covert features. The screen print appears fluorescent blue, whereas green/yellow emissions could be detected upon the usage of UV/green block filters. These features facilitate straightforward real-life applications including verification of prints by authorized users using simple tools such as UV and green light filters, while simultaneously complicating the replication task for counterfeiters.

CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal approach by dissolving 50 mg of TTP in a mixture of 5 mL Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 1 mL ethanol to achieve a homogeneous solution. The reaction solution was then adjusted to 10 mL using DMSO in a Teflon-coated autoclave, and the reaction was conducted at 180℃ for various durations. The resulting mother liquors (ML) obtained after 1, 3, 5, and 7 h, labelled as ML-1, ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 respectively were filtered to remove suspended solids. The column for purifying TTP-CDs was prepared by wet packing using 400–700 mesh silica gel obtained from SRL Chemicals.

Absorption and emission spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and a JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300 instrument, with excitation source and emission bandwidths set between 2.5 and 1 nm. Liquid chromatography-Mass spectrometry (LC-MS) data was acquired from an ACQUITY UPLC H-Class PLUS Bio System. CDs dispersion (0.1 mL) was injected into a liquid chromatography column type ACQUITY UPLC® BEH C18 with a mesh size of 1.7 µm. The binary solvent system comprised solvent A as water with 0.1 % formic acid and solvent B as methanol. The end of the column was attached to a UV detector for component separation with excitation at 415 nm, followed by analysis via positive electrospray ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry. 13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Distortionless Enhancement by Polarization Transfer (DEPT)-135 spectra were recorded using Bruker Ascend equipment. Fluorescence lifetime values were measured in nanoseconds using a DeltaFlex Time Correlated Single Photon count (TCSPC) Lifetime Fluorimeter equipped with a conventional photomultiplier tube detector set with 13 ps per bin (channel) resolution and an instrument response function of ∼ 70 ps. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM)/HRTEM images and Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained using a JEM 2100F model with a 200 kV electron source. Characteristic functional groups were identified using an Attenuated total reflectance- Infrared (ATR-IR) Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer with 40 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded using a Rigaku Miniflex 600 (5th gen) instrument. Elements were detected using an Omicron X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) system (Nanotechnology Ltd., Germany) with non-monochromatic Mg-Kα and Al-Kα as the excitation sources. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw Compact Raman Spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm for an exposure time of 30 s. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images were captured using a ZEISS EVO SEM and GEMINI 300, Carl Zeiss High-Resolution Field Emission Gun SEM.

The computational studies were conducted on four optimized structures of CDs to investigate their photophysical and chemical properties. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using B3LYP theory and a Gaussian type 6-31G(d,p) basis set. Initially, the structures were optimized, followed by harmonic frequency calculations to confirm their positions at the minimum of the potential energy surface. The UV–Visible spectra of CDs were calculated by vertical excitation of the optimized molecule in the ground electronic state, considering one hundred possible excited states. For the emission spectrum, calculations were carried out from the 1st to the 30th excited states. All calculations were performed in methanol environment. The Gaussian 09 program suite was employed for all computations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples for aggregation and pH studies
ce:section.ce:para: To conduct aggregation studies on TTP-CDs, a Tetrahydrofuran (THF)/water solvent system ranging from 0 to 99 % water content was prepared. A few aliquots (0.5 mL) of dispersed CDs were added, and the total volume was maintained at 5 mL. Various solutions with pH ranging from 1 to 12 were prepared by adding varying volumes of glacial acetic acid and 1 M NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of flexo/screen ink and study of print properties
ce:section.ce:para: The solvent (methanol) was evaporated from the fifth fraction to obtain TTP-CDs, which were re-dispersed in de-ionized water. Ink coating on the paper substrate was achieved with a zero number K bar coater. Flexography printing was conducted using an RK Flexiproof 100 at 50 m/min, 9.5 BCM Anilox roll, chambered doctor blade inking equipment, and a photopolymer plate. For the preparation of screen ink, TTP-CD dispersion was added to the screen medium (serves as binder), along with cyclohexanone and THF as solvent/co-solvent to control the viscosity of ink formulation. Screen prints were obtained using a screen mesh with a 120-mesh count.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and column purification of TTP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of ML-1, ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 samples obtained at varying reaction durations were analysed and compared with the absorption profile of TTP. The precursor (Figure Supplementary Fig. S1a) primarily absorbs in the visible region, exhibiting a maximum absorption peak at 420 nm, known as the Soret band, along with less intense peaks between 520–650 nm, referred to as Q-bands [40]. Interestingly, all ML samples demonstrate similar absorbance patterns (Fig. S1b–e) to that of the precursor, suggesting the presence of comparable functional groups after the chemical transformation. Additionally, a new absorption peak is discerned at lower wavelengths, ranging from 264-270 nm, which could potentially stem from either new electronic transition within the molecule or interactions with the solvent.

Thin layer chromatography (TLC) separations were conducted to assess the completion of the solvothermal reaction. Various solvent systems including ethyl acetate:hexane, dichloromethane (DCM):methanol, and diethyl ether:petroleum ether were tested. The DCM:methanol proved to be the most effective for component separation. Initially, non-polar DCM was employed, gradually transitioning to more polar elution mixtures to optimize separation efficiency. As depicted in Fig. S2a–d, TLC separations were performed for ML-1, ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 samples.

To ensure complete adsorption, several aliquots of ML were introduced at the top of the column. The solvent systems optimized via TLC were employed, starting with non-polar solvent and gradually increasing the polarity. The Fig. S3a–d illustrates the chromatographic separations corresponding to ML-1, ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 samples. Chromatography facilitated the elution of fractions based on polarity differences, with the entire process completed within 2 h. The Fig. S4a presents the elution times of different fractions from the column, revealing that the fourth fraction exhibited a longer retention time compared to the preceding fractions. While the first, second, and third fractions were miscible in DCM, the fourth fraction displayed miscibility in methanol, as depicted in Fig. S4b–i.

The UV spectra of the purified fractions eluted from all ML samples are depicted in Fig. 1 a–d. The peaks belonging to higher and lower energy transitions could be assigned to π→π* and n→π*. All four fractions of ML-1 display peaks at 275 and 260 nm (Fig. 1a). Additionally, these fractions exhibit absorbance in the region of 400–432 nm, which could be attributed to the soret band, akin to what is observed in the precursor molecule [41]. Furthermore, all four fractions from ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 samples display similar bands to those observed in the precursor. Thus, it can be inferred that similar functional groups from the precursor are retained in the resultant solvothermal product, and variations in reaction duration do not yield additional peaks.

The emission features of TTP can be understood well from the 3D photoluminescence (PL) spectra where the major emissions are centred at 650 and 720 nm for excitation at 420 nm (Fig. S5a). However, the 2D PL spectrum demonstrates that similar emissions are obtained for excitations at 370, 420, 520, 550, and 590 nm (Fig. S5b). Upon subsequent solvothermal treatment, all the ML samples (ML-1, 3, 5, and 7) display similar emission behaviour with the presence of additional fluorophores. Some fluorophores remain unmodified, which shows similar emissions as those of precursor. Additional emissive species corresponding to 530 and 570 nm are also formed as evident from the Fig. S5c–g, which suggests certain chemical modifications after the reaction. The first and second fractions of ML-1 (Fig. S6a–d) that elute initially showed a composition with greater proportion of unmodified fluorophores (λem = 650 & 720 nm). However, a gradual increase in the polarity of the solvent induces elution of the third and fourth fractions (Fig. S6e–h) which display a greater proportion of modified fluorophores (λem = 530 & 570 nm). In contrast, all three fractions of ML-3 samples demonstrate a higher composition of unmodified fluorophores compared to modified fluorophores, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a–d. The first and second eluents have emitters corresponding to 650 & 720 nm. The latter polar fractions (third & fourth) showcased the presence of both unmodified and modified fluorophores in comparable proportions. The PL spectra of the first, second, and third fractions of ML-5 (Fig. S7a–d) and ML-7 (Fig. S7e–h) illustrate the initial elution of a greater concentration of unmodified fluorophore (λem = 650 & 720 nm). Meanwhile, the latter (fourth) fraction of ML-5 (Fig. S7d) and ML-7 (Fig. S7h) exhibit a comparable composition of both fluorophores, indicating similar emission properties. Therefore, it can be inferred that the fourth fraction of all the MLs contain a higher proportion of modified fluorophores and were selected for further QY studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The particle sizes of all the ML samples and their constituent column purified fractions are acquired as shown in Fig. S8a–d. The ML-1 sample possesses particles with sizes both < 10 nm and > 1000 nm (Fig. S8a). Similar observations are recorded for the ML-3 sample (Fig. S8b). However, ML 5 & 7 displays majorly particles of > 10 nm size (Fig. S8c and d). The column-purified fractions of ML-1 have size distributions in descending order with the last fraction of smaller particle distribution (<100 nm). However, this trend is not exhibited by purified fractions of other ML samples. The fourth fractions of the ML 3–7 sample have particle sizes beyond 100 nm indicating its polydisperse nature. Another underlying factor that affects particle size is the surface charge. The Zeta potential of all the ML samples portrays varying results suggesting the presence of diverse groups (Fig. S9a–d). However, ML-1, 3 and 7 exhibits zeta potential values which suggest minimal colloidal stability. The Zeta potential values of fourth fractions of ML 1 and 3 have a certain degree of colloidal stability making storage possible under ambient conditions. The individual particle size (nm) and zeta potential (mV) for ML 1–7 and constituent purified samples are listed in Table supplementary Table S1–S4.

The fluorescence QY provides the inherent ability of a fluorophore to emit light upon absorbing a photon. Hence, the QY of TTP was compared with those of all MLs and their separated fractions. The precursor exhibited a QY of 35.37 %. Surprisingly, a significant decrease in QYs was observed after solvothermal treatment: ML-1, ML-3, ML-5, and ML-7 displayed QY values of 10.23 %, 9.77 %, 9.59 %, and 13.23 %, respectively. Following column separations of all four ML samples, the highest QY value of 38.85 % was determined for the fourth fraction from ML-3, while the fourth fractions of ML-1, ML-5, and ML-7 exhibited 3.61 %, 11.09 %, and 15.01 %, respectively. These QY values are graphically represented in Fig. S10 and the respective values are listed in Table S5.

The size distribution of all the ML and constituent fractions presented diverse particle distribution in the dispersed state. However, the extent and efficiency of column separation can be examined via TEM imaging. The low-resolution TEM images of ML-3 enabled to view several nanosheets (Fig. S11a) in addition to carbon nanoparticles including CDs. During imaging, the first, second and third fractions of ML-3 are seen to possess greater composition of CNTs (Fig. S11b), carbon nanosheets (Fig. S11c) and carbon nanofibers (Fig. S11d) along with CDs (Fig. S11e–i) in all fractions. Based on this result and several literatures it can be inferred that during the solvothermal synthesis of CDs, several carbon nanomaterials are formed as by-products. The size histogram plots corresponding to the first, second and third fractions depict CDs with sizes between 1–20 nm (Fig. S11k–m). Nevertheless, the fourth fraction displays numerous CDs (Fig. S11e and Fig. 3 a) having an average diameter of 3–5 nm (Fig. 3b). The SAED pattern recorded (Fig. 3c) for a selected area shows bright spots and ring diffraction indicating the polycrystallinity of the CDs. The lattice infringes observed in HR-TEM (Fig. 3d) imaging were measured to be 0.16 nm. Moreover, the TEM-EDS scan (Fig. 3e) performed over a specific area further proves oxygen as the populous element. The Fig. 3f corresponds to the HAADF image displaying the elemental distribution along with elemental mapping of individual elements such as Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), Oxygen (O) and Sulphur (S) as depicted in the Fig. 3g–j. The elemental mapping reveals O as the most abundant element, followed by C and S, which exhibit comparable distributions. On the other hand, N is present in minor amounts, with sparse distribution.

Further to assess the complete conversion of TTP after solvothermal reaction LC-MS analysis was performed for ML-3. Fig. S12A presents the chromatogram and the mass spectra of different eluents. There is no peak for m/z = 670.8 corresponding to TTP indicating its complete degradation upon solvothermal treatment. Moreover, chromatogram and mass spectra for the first, second, third (Fig. S12B–D) and fourth fractions were also acquired (Fig. 4 ). The mass spectra of column separated fractions do not possess the m/z peak corresponding to the precursor suggesting its complete degradation during synthesis. The chromatogram (Fig. 4a) of the fourth fraction CDs of interest with the highest QY reveals eight peaks corresponding to different constituents present in the fourth fraction of ML-3, each with its respective retention time. Interestingly, all eight mass spectra (Fig. 4b–i) demonstrate the absence of a base peak beyond 410.1, indicating the successful degradation of TTP during the synthesis process.

As the fourth fraction of ML-3 displayed mainly CDs (TTP-CDs) with the highest QY, this CD fraction was chosen for further studies. The lifetime studies for the TTP-CDs were performed in both liquid and solid state with different excitations. For excitation at shorter wavelengths (400, 430 and 450 nm), the plots showed multiexponential decay (Fig. S13a–c) with shorter average lifetime values. While for excitation at longer wavelengths (480 and 520 nm), the CDs displayed bi-exponential decay (Fig. S14a and b) with small increment in average lifetime values. Due to the diverse chemical environment or multiple emissive centres on the surface of CDs, they exhibit different individual lifetime values. The use of a short-wavelength excitation source induces excitation of species with a faster decay rate or smaller lifetime value. Moreover, the use of longer wavelength source causes the excitation of species with slower decay. Although, the emitters corresponding to 530 (1.00, 1.14, 1.80 ns) and 570 nm (0.90, 1.55, 2.40 ns) display variations in lifetime for excitations at 400, 430 and 450 nm, the longer wavelength emitter (λem = 650 nm) display longer lifetime (4.63, 2.63, 1.71 ns) in comparison to 530 and 570 nm. The individual and average lifetime values of CDs in liquid state are computed for excitations at 400, 430, 450, 480 and 520 nm and listed in the Table S6 and Table S7.

The CDs exhibit small decline in lifetime for emitters corresponding to 540 nm and a significant decline for emitters belonging to 580 and 650 nm. Thus, we can conclude that in the solid state, CDs corresponding to longer wavelengths are more susceptible to quenching, leading to a faster decay rate. This may be attributed to the restricted rotation of bonds and excessive resonance energy transfer in the solid state, resulting in a decline in lifetime values [42]. The lifetime plots for paper samples coated with CDs (λem = 540, 580 & 650 nm) exhibit multiexponential decay for excitations at 370, 400 and 430 nm as shown in Fig. S15a-c. For excitation pertaining to 520 nm the longer wavelength emitter (λem = 650 nm) shows mono-exponential decay (Fig. S15d). The individual and average lifetime values of coated CDs for excitations recorded at 370, 400, 430 and 520 nm are computed in the Table S8.

The XRD spectrum for the CDs (Fig. 5 a) was obtained by drop casting the dispersion on a glass slide. The plot presents a broad and sharp peak which is compared with graphitic carbon as reference (Table S9). The diffraction peaks centered at 27.1, 31.4, 45.3, 56.3 and 75.0 partly coincides with [002], [111], [121], [212] and [204] diffraction planes of reference suggesting an arrangement in closer proximity to graphitic carbon. The Fig. S16 depicts the XRD spectrum for the glass slide depicting an amorphous peak at 23.6. The Raman spectra of precursor, DMSO and CDs are depicted in Fig. 5b and their Raman shift values are provided in Table S10. The Raman spectrum for the precursor exhibits peaks at 1011 cm−1 attributed to pyrrole ring breathing mode. The peak at 1277 cm−1 results from the contribution of in-plane deformations of C-Ph (Phenyl group substituent attached to pyrrole group) and C-N stretching vibrations. The peak at 1598 cm−1 corresponds to C–C asymmetric stretch in phenyl groups [43,44]. The Raman spectrum for DMSO is also recorded using a quartz plate which displays characteristic peaks from 1460-1800 cm−1. The spectral peaks at 955, 1036, 1313 and 1421 cm−1 accounts for CH3 bend, S=O stretch, C–H symmetric and CH3 degenerate deformations [45]. The CDs display a peak at 1172 cm−1 due to C–H deformations and the peak centered at 1377 cm−1 and 1622 cm−1 are assigned as D-band and G-band labeled as distorted and graphitic bands and appears due to sp3 and sp2 carbon groups [46,47].

The XPS analysis provides the elemental composition and hybridisation of the elements. The survey spectrum (Fig. 5c) presents peaks at 160.3, 290.6, 400.9, and 536.2 eV which are assigned for S2p (S), C1s (C), N1s (N) and O1s (O) [48]. The elemental composition depicts O (61.64 %) as major element followed by C (14.28 %), S (12.3 %), K (8.59 %) and N (3.17 %). The substantial changes in carbon to oxygen ratios compared to TTP precursor suggests significant chemical transformations. Deconvolution is performed on the full scan spectra of C1s (Fig. 5d) to fit three peaks at 286.1, 286.6 eV and 290.4 eV assigned for C–C/C=C, C-O/C-N/C-S and O=C-O groups respectively. The peaks obtained after deconvolution of O1s spectra (Fig. 5e) centered at 533.8 and 535.5 eV belong to C=O and C-O groups. Furthermore, 13C NMR (Fig. S17a) and DEPT-135 (Fig. S17b) spectral traces were collected, revealing various carbon groups in different hybridization states, further validating the XPS results. The peaks observed at 164.5 and 128.6 ppm can be assigned to carbonyl (C=O) and aromatic carbons. Moreover, peaks between 17.5–63.9 ppm correspond to aliphatic carbon (C–C) and those attached to electronegative groups. The DEPT-135 spectrum that gives signals of all protonated carbons was also recorded, which shows positive peaks confirming the presence of tertiary (CH) and primary (CH3) carbons, while a negative signal at 57.2 ppm indicates secondary (CH2) group [49].

The FT-IR spectrum of TTP powder displays several peaks between 738, 790 and 839 cm−1 ascribed to N–H (out-of-plane) deformations, C–H bend of phenyl and methyl group (Fig. 5f). Several peaks are observed at 1012–1172 cm−1 corresponding to C–H stretch in pyrrole groups. In addition, the peak at 1366 and 1512 cm−1 belongs to C-N and C=C stretching vibrations. The precursor exhibits weak vinyl C–H stretch at 3030 and 3118 cm−1 [50]. The FT-IR spectra of ML and CDs were recorded in DMSO and methanol as solvent, respectively. DMSO demonstrates sharp peak at 670, 950 and 1035–1070 cm−1 attributed to C-S-C bend, CH3 bend and S=O stretching vibrations [51,52]. The resultant ML obtained after solvothermal treatment displays numerous peaks at 1260, 1387, 1512, 1663 cm−1 belonging to C-N, CH3, C=C and C=O stretching vibrations. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs, the peak at 657 cm−1 is due to C-S-C bending vibration. The peak corresponding to = C–H group for aromatic ring deformations is observed at 950 cm−1 while peak at 1020 cm−1 is due to C-O–H bending vibration. In addition, the peaks observed at 1099 cm−1 corresponds collectively to C-O-C for 6 membered cyclic group and S=O stretch. The peaks at 1260, 1387, 1512, 1661, 2831 and 2941 cm−1 are due to C-O stretch, CH3 bending, C=C and C=O stretching, symmetric and asymmetric C–H stretching vibrations. Moreover, a broad peak is observed at 3358 cm−1 which may arise due to hydrogen bonding between N–H group with the –OH group of methanol. The Table S11 lists the characteristic wavenumber (cm−1) values of precursor (both solid and dispersed in DMSO), ML and fourth fraction.

DFT studies were conducted to determine potential structures of TTP-CDs and to theoretically deduce UV, FT-IR and PL spectra. During the synthesis, diverse CDs with varying structures and functional groups are formed. While the results obtained through theoretical methods may vary from experimental conditions due to several factors, computational analysis of CD structures at this level is conducted to gain insight into their potential configurations. Based on harmonic frequency calculations, four optimized structures (Fig. 6 a–d) containing C, O, S, and N atoms are deduced. The theoretically derived FT-IR spectra for four structures (Fig. 6e–h) exhibit a common absorption peak, which aligns with the experimental FT-IR plot of CDs. The first structure (Fig. 6e) displays FT-IR peaks which overlap well with designated peaks for the functional groups present in CDs. The spectral peaks acquired for the second structure partly overlap with the experimental data (Fig. 6f). However, the FT-IR plots deduced for third and fourth structure display significant shift which can arise due to intramolecular interactions (Fig. 6g and h). Based on these findings, we speculate that the FT-IR spectrum for the first structure closely resembles spectral peaks of CDs. However, the peaks are an indication of the presence of specific functional groups and their prominent appearances lie in strength of bonds, hydrogen bonding or solvent interactions. Hence, it is possible that CDs with chemical structures and functional groups similar to those of structures two, three, and four may constitute a minor portion of the population and thus may not be prominently observed during experimental FT-IR measurements.

Absorption studies are conducted to identify the predominant transitions attributed to various chemical groups present in the CDs. The experimental UV spectrum displays intense absorbance peaks at 390 and 420 nm, representing the major transitions, while bands at 520 and 550 nm, corresponds to minor transitions. These results are compared with all four theoretically derived UV plots. In case of the first structure, the absorption peaks at 390 and 423 nm correspond well with the major transitions observed in UV spectra of CDs (Fig. 6i). The absorbance peaks observed in second and third structures (Fig. 6j and k) partly overlap with the absorption peaks of CDs. However, the absorbance of structure four (Fig. 6l) displays a small blue-shift corresponding to the same transitions. On the contrary, the minor transitions obtained around 500–600 nm in the UV spectra of CDs are also observed in the absorption spectra of structures two, three and four. It is difficult to deduce the exact fluorescence mechanism at this level of DFT calculations [53,54]​. To obtain emission spectra for the theoretically derived structures, calculations are conducted spanning from the 1st to the 30th excited states. Since the major absorption of TTP-CDs corresponding to 25-30th excited state is around 390 and 420 nm, we were able to obtain one emission spectrum centred at 570 nm (Fig. S18) which agrees with one of the experimental emission peaks.

CDs which are either weakly emissive in dispersed state and become highly emissive in aggregated state or with quenched emission due to higher concentration or in aggregate form are reported in literature [55–60]. Fig. 7 a depicts CDs dispersed in various solvents and viewed under daylight and 365 nm UV source. Initially the PL spectra were recorded for CDs soluble in different aprotic and protic solvents as shown in the Fig. 7b–d and Fig. S19a–c. The addition of polar protic solvents induces reduction in fluorescence intensity of 650 and 720 nm emissions, while enhancement in emission intensity for 400 and 430 nm (λex = 370 nm) emissions. To explore this observation, the CDs were dispersed in a good solvent (i.e THF) and increased proportion of a poor solvent (i.e water) was added and the photographs of these solutions with varying water content is shown in Fig. 7e. Further, the emissions at 400–530 nm are monitored for 370 nm excitation (Fig. 7f). Furthermore, inherent excitations of CDs at 400 and 430 nm were used to monitor emissions at 650–720 nm (Fig. 7g and h). The increment in the water ratios induces a steady decline in the emissions corresponding to 430 nm. However, the emission peaks at 650 and 720 nm display maximum intensity corresponding to 50 % water and a decline in fluorescence intensity beyond 70 % water content. On the contrary, the CDs dispersed in 80 % water displayed increment in emission intensity. The dramatic changes in the emission intensities could be due to interactions of the surface functional groups of the CDs. Therefore, to gather adequate understanding of interactions of surface groups of CDs, 13C NMR spectra were recorded for different water fractions. The NMR spectrum of CDs dispersed in 0 % water (Fig. S20a) or 100 % THF exhibits carbonyl carbons resonating at 162.4 ppm, the aromatic carbons spanning from 104.2-137.6 ppm and aliphatic carbons ranging from 30.5-74.2 ppm. Addition of water results in weakening of peaks corresponding to aromatic group (60–70 % water) (Fig. S21b and Fig. S21a) and further addition of water induces its complete disappearance (80–90 % water) of peaks (Fig. S20c and d). Based on this finding, we speculate that addition of water causes π-π stacking [61]. The TEM image (Fig. S21b) for the CDs dispersed in 80 % water are also acquired which shows clusters or aggregates with their size ranging between 5–40 nm (Fig. S21c). The SAED pattern (Fig. S21d) corresponds to graphitic CDs and indicates that lattice d-spaces remain unchanged. Therefore, we conclude the formation of cluster or aggregates of CDs upon addition of water and not the presence of larger sized carbon nanoparticles.

The formation of aggregates is also governed by the pH of the medium in addition to the surface functional groups and polarity [62]. The hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs in different pH solutions was measured using dynamic light scattering experiments as portrayed in Fig. S22. The photographs as shown in Fig. 8 a depict the CDs dispersed in pH 1–12 solutions under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination. It is observed that at neutral pH the particle size is < 90 nm, but the CDs dispersion display broader size distributions in pH solutions of 2, 3, 6 and 12. Further, the interaction of H+/OH− of the medium with surface groups of CDs are studied to obtain a co-relation of zeta-potential with their hydrodynamic size (Fig. 8b). The surface charges on CDs dispersed in pH 1 and 2 have zeta potential values closer to zero with larger size profiles. Neutral surface charge can result in loss of colloidal stability leading to formation of aggregates. Moreover, the vibrational peaks in the FT-IR spectrum corresponding to the hydroxyl groups of the CDs have higher wave number indicative of limited solvent interactions which results from aggregate formation (Fig. S23). However, at pH 3, broader size distribution (Fig. S22) with negative zeta potential value is observed for the nanoparticles (Fig. 8b). This could be due to the formation of hydration sphere around the surface of CDs. Also, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs in this pH exhibits a broad peak at 3200 cm−1 indicating intermolecular interactions of hydroxyl groups with the medium (Fig. S23). The CDs in pH 6 have zeta potential closer to zero (Fig. 8b) and are in colloidal instability zone. This results in aggregate formation due to larger size distribution.

At neutral pH of 7, CDs possess a zeta potential of −3.9 mV due to the presence of numerous negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl surface groups. At higher pH (8–10), deprotonation of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups is likely to occur as evident from increased negative zeta potential value. However, at pH 12 the overall surface charge is closer to zero. Due to colloidal instability, the CDs are more prone to form aggregate as evident from their broad size distribution. The formation of aggregates of higher particle size is visible in the form of precipitates as seen in Fig. 8a. Table S12 provides FT-IR peak values of CDs dispersed in different pH solutions.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs in all pH solutions at different excitation wavelengths were collected (Fig. S24 and Fig. 8c–f) Interestingly, the fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersed in all pH solutions (Fig. S24a and b) recorded at 370 and 400 nm excitations displayed maximum emission at 460 and 480 nm. However, the use of excitations at 430 and 440 nm (Fig. 8c and d) depict prominent shift from 700 to 720 nm for CDs dispersed in solutions of pH 1–5. CDs in solutions of pH 6–12 exhibit major emissions confined between 480–540 nm. The emission spectra recorded at excitations corresponding to 550 and 590 nm (Fig. S24c and d) also portrays similar observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of inks and analysis of print proofs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the CDs were coated on UV dull paper by simple drop casting method. The 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. S25a) of coated CDs show emissions at 440 and 470 nm (marked as A) for excitation at 370 nm. In addition, CDs displayed maximum emission at 650 and 720 nm for excitation at 430 nm (marked as B). A red shift of 10 nm in emission to 540 and 580 nm (marked as C and D) is observed from CDs coated on paper compared to 530 and 570 nm when in liquid state. To differentiate the emissions from UV dull paper alone the fluorescence spectrum of blank paper was recorded. For excitation at 370 nm and 400 nm, the paper exhibits emissions at 440, 470 and 530 nm. The 2D fluorescence spectra for the coated CDs recorded at the same excitations (Fig. S25b) exhibit emissions at 540, 580, 650 and 720 nm which correspond to CDs alone. Similar emissions are observed for corresponding excitations at 430, 440, 500, 520, 550 and 590 nm. The same excitations provided to UV dull paper did not yield any emission peak beyond 530 nm (Fig. S25c). Further, the as-synthesized CDs were used as a fluorescent colorant in formulating both water and solvent based inks for flexography and screen printing, respectively. Solvent based inks require relatively less drying time, whereas water-based inks offer a suitable approach to alleviate environmental concerns [63]. The print properties were studied as the CDs in different chemical environment could exhibit unique print features.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of flexo-ink and evaluation of print proof properties
ce:section.ce:para: Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) serves as an ideal binder for water-based ink formulation mainly due to its film forming ability upon drying [64]. Initially PVA granules were dissolved in distilled water and heated at 90℃ for 1 h. Then CD dispersion was added and sonicated for homogeneity to obtain the water-based ink formulation with the final composition; 16.53 wt% of pigment concentrate (CDs dispersion), 23.84 % of 40 % PVA and 59.63 % of water. The transparent control formulation devoid of CDs displays pale blue fluorescence under UV light source (Fig. S26a), whereas the prepared off-white flexo ink emitted pale pink fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (Fig. S26b). The 3D fluorescence spectrum recorded for the flexo ink depicts major emissions at 650 nm (marked as B) for excitation at 420 nm as shown in Fig. 9 a. The 2D PL spectrum shows emissions at 430, 460, 540, 570, 650 and 720 nm for excitation at 370 nm (Fig. 9b) while emissions at 540, 570, 650 and 720 nm are observed for excitations at 430, 440, 470 and 520 nm respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of control formulation displays maximum emissions at 430, 460 and 480 nm for excitations at 370 and 400 nm, while there are no emissions beyond 500 nm. This further proves that emissions between 530–720 nm belong to CDs, without any interference from the binder (Fig. 9c).

The ink and the control formulation were used to obtain print proofs on UV dull paper via flexography (Fig. S26c and d). Comparison of the SEM images of UV dull paper (Fig. S27a and b) and flexo print proofs (Fig. S27c and d) indicate that the ink is uniformly spread on the UV dull paper. The fluorescence spectrum of the flexo printed sample (Fig. 9d) shows emissions at 650 nm (marked as B) as observed in the flexo ink. Its 2D spectrum shows major emissions at 440 and 470 nm for excitations at 370 and 400 nm in addition to less intense emission peaks at 650 and 720 (as shown in inset of Fig. 9e). Moreover, the use of inherent excitations of CDs corresponding to 430, 520, 550 and 590 nm produces similar emission peaks. However, the print proof from control formulation shows emissions at 440 and 530 nm for excitation at 370 and 400 nm (Fig. 9f). Moreover, the use of inherent excitations of the CDs do not induce emissions beyond 530 nm. This indicates that emission at 440 and 530 nm belongs to control formulation and emissions at 570, 650 and 720 nm are due to the CDs.

To check the photostability of the ink, the print proofs were cut (2.5 cm × 2.5 cm) and passed through UV curing unit at the rate of 300 W/ 30 m /min for different number of exposure cycles. The emission spectra of the UV exposed print proofs were monitored for excitations at 370, 400, 430, 520, 550 and 590 nm as shown in Fig. S28a–f. The change in fluorescence intensity was monitored for emissions at 650 and 720 nm (which belong exclusively to the CDs). UV exposures do not bring considerable changes in the emission intensities which could indicate the photostability of the print.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of screen ink and evaluation of print proof properties
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the solvent-based ink formulation for screen printing with desired viscosity, the screen medium (binder), cyclohexanone (solvent) and THF (co-solvent) were used. The final ink with the following composition (weight %); 20.73 % pigment concentrate (CDs dispersion), 68.27 % screen medium and 11.00 % THF-Cyclohexanone (solvent/co-solvent) was formulated. The pale-yellow screen ink displays pale-yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination, while the colourless control formulation exhibits weak blue fluorescence (Fig. S29a and b). The 3D fluorescence spectrum of screen ink also shows major emissions at 650 and 720 nm (marked as B) for excitation corresponding to 420 nm as shown in the Fig. 10 a and the 2D spectra shows emissions at 540, 570, 650 and 720 nm for excitations at 490 and 520 nm (Fig. 10b). The control formulation (Fig. 10c) exhibit emissions confined to 460–530 nm region indicating that emissions beyond 530 nm are from the CDs alone. The screen print proofs were obtained by placing UV dull paper below the screen mesh and applying the screen ink using polyurethane squeegee. The SEM images of the screen print proof (Fig. S27e and f) indicate the formation of a uniform inked region on the UV dull paper. The screen print appears invisible under daylight (Fig. S29c) and displays blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV source (Fig. S29d). The 3D and 2D PL spectra of screen print proofs exhibit major emissions at 650 and 720 nm (marked as B) upon 430 nm excitation (Fig. 10d and e). To distinguish the emissions from the binder and solvents, the emission spectrum of control formulation was also recorded at 430 nm excitation (Fig. 10f), which exhibited emissions confined within 520 nm, proving that emissions beyond 520 nm from the screen print are contributed by the CDs alone.

The experiments for checking the photostability of screen print proofs were performed by UV curing unit at the rate of 300 W/ 30 m /min for different exposure cycles and the fluorescence spectra are portrayed in Fig. S30a–f. The emission peaks at 650 and 720 nm were monitored and upon exposure to 10 cycles, emission intensities were found to decline. However, further exposure to UV cycles did not produce any substantial quenching in fluorescence intensities. Interestingly, a new emission peak at 690 nm for excitations at 454 nm is observed from the UV exposed print (Fig. S30g). To test the distinctiveness of this peak, the control printed samples were also subjected to similar UV passes. The fluorescence spectra recorded at similar excitation for the UV exposed control print do not replicate any peak at 690 nm, which provides clear evidence for the origin of the emission from CDs dispersed in screen medium. To further comprehend the origin of this new emission peak, morphological changes of the screen-printed area before and after UV passes were examined after drop casting the ink on UV dull papers. The acquired FESEM images of the dried solvent-based ink appear porous (Fig. S31a and b) while post-UV exposure (40 passes) appears less porous and more uniform (Fig. S31c and d). From these results we assume that the UV exposure may cause hardening of the ink film.

UV fluorescent ink prints offer an effective as well as economical solution for obtaining invisible codes, texts, and logos for various security applications as a smart alternative to complex and costly anticounterfeit solutions. Hong and yang prepared blue emissive CDs from wasted coffee grounds and sprayed on bank notes. The ink exhibited covert features as it was invisible under daylight and fluorescent blue under UV light [65]. Li and team synthesized CDs from natural plant dyes and printed on cotton fabric via inkjet printing. The print was invisible under daylight while under 365 nm and 395 nm illumination the patterns appeared distinctly fluorescent [66,67]. Yang et.al developed ink formulation using xylose derived CDs and printed on filter paper. Under daylight the QR code appeared golden colour and displayed green fluorescence under UV source, which shifted to a base-induced cyan fluorescence. The initial green fluorescence was re-introduced by the application of dilute acetic acid coating [68]. Another security ink was developed enabling fluorescence imaging and lifetime encoded decryption mode anti-counterfeit technique [69]. The ink was applied on non-fluorescent paper which displayed blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Furthermore, fluorescence lifetime imaging enabled to extract specific lifetime values encoded pixels and served as unique information encoding technique. Liu and group utilised core-shelled QDs in inkjet ink formulation and printed on ITO coated glass slides [9]. The characteristic fluorescence imaging was stored in AI database which enabled the user to identify the counterfeit product.

The current study unfolds the unique emission features observed in the print proofs generated using solvent based formulation with TTP-CDs. On a UV dull paper, ‘MAHE’ word was inscribed using the solvent-based ink by screen printing technique. The print proofs appeared pale yellow in daylight. However, under 370, 400 and 430 nm excitations the printed letters appeared bright fluorescent blue. In similar scenario, the forger may perceive the inscribed letters as blue fluorescent and similar duplications may be performed. The prints can be authenticated by the user through visualization under two filters: (a) 430 nm and (b) 520 nm block filters (Fig. 10g). The user can view the print sample as cyan blue (λex = 400 nm) and pale yellow (λex = 430 nm) under 430 nm block filter. Further under long pass filter, the red emissions can be viewed under both 400 and 430 nm excitations. Thus, the forger observes only blue fluorescence from the prints, whereas the user can check the authenticity of the prints by using the two filters to observe ‘MAHE’ in different fluorescence colours when excited at 400 and 430 nm. This feature can be well-utilized as an additional level of security against forgery of security documents.

The conclusions of the present study underscore the practicality and effectiveness of UV fluorescent ink prints using column purified TTP-CDs as a viable alternative for enhancing security measures, thus addressing the persistent challenge of counterfeit prevention in various applications. The unique emission features of the prints, particularly the bright blue fluorescence observed under UV excitation and the pale-yellow fluorescence under longer wavelengths, provide a distinctive method for authentication of prints. By employing different filters, users can discern different fluorescence colors, allowing for easy verification of the authenticity of security documents. This additional layer of security adds complexity for forgers to replicate, while ensuring accessibility and simplicity for users.
